citation.proposition: A hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.reasoning: Privette v. Superior Court, 5 Cal.4th 689 (1993) is the seminal California Supreme Court case establishing the 'Privette doctrine,' which holds that a hirer of an independent contractor is generally not liable for injuries to the contractor's employees.
citations.confidence_reasoning: This is a foundational case in California tort law.

citation.proposition: A hirer is never liable for injuries sustained by an independent contractor's employees when the injuries arise from the contracted work.

citations.reasoning: While the case exists, the proposition is legally inaccurate. The Privette doctrine is not absolute; there are recognized exceptions (e.g., the 'peculiar risk' doctrine, retained control, or non-delegable duties). The use of the word 'never' misstates the law.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The California Supreme Court explicitly acknowledges exceptions to the Privette doctrine in subsequent cases like Hooker v. Department of Transportation.

citation.text: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)
citation.proposition: Granting summary judgment to hirer where subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citations.reasoning: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189 (C.D. Cal. 2004) is a real case that applied the Privette doctrine to grant summary judgment based on the subcontractor's control and the inherent risks of the work.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The case citation and holding match standard legal databases for this specific district court decision.

citation.text: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)
citation.case_name: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
citation.proposition: Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

citations.reasoning: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc. at 887 F.2d 1204 does not appear to exist. A search of federal reporters does not yield this case name or citation combination. Furthermore, OSHA standards generally do not create a rebuttable presumption of due care in private tort litigation.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The citation format is standard, but the case is not found in legal research databases.

citation.text: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011)
citation.case_name: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
citation.proposition: Compliance with statutory safety requirements is highly probative of the exercise of due care.

citations.reasoning: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590 (2011) exists, but it actually held that a hirer is not liable for injuries to an independent contractor's employee even if the hirer failed to comply with safety regulations, unless the hirer affirmatively contributed to the injury.
citations.confidence_reasoning: Seabright is a well-known California Supreme Court case that limits the application of negligence per se in the context of the Privette doctrine.

facts.claim: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
